sections.title: Start with the execution model
sections.body: Python reads top to bottom, but the important part is understanding what each line changes. Variables hold references, functions package behavior, and control flow decides what runs next.
sections.tip: Trace the value after each line, not just the final output.

sections.title: Strings, slicing, and f-strings
sections.body: Strings are sequences. That means slicing works the same way as with lists, and f-strings are the cleanest way to format values when you already know the variables you want to print.
sections.tip: Remember: slicing excludes the end index.

sections.title: Input and output
sections.body: input() always returns text. If you need numbers, cast explicitly. print() is for displaying output; return is for sending a value back to the caller.
sections.tip: Most beginner bugs come from forgetting the type conversion.

sections.title: Ranges and loops
sections.body: range(start, stop, step) stops before stop. That off-by-one detail matters everywhere you loop, slice, or index arrays.
sections.tip: If you can predict the exact output, you understand the loop.

keyTerms.definition: A name that points at a value.
Text data like "hello".
A way to take part of a sequence.
A reusable block of code that does one job.
A sequence of numbers used in loops.

python-basics.feynmanPrompt: Explain why Python uses indentation and why input() often needs int() before you do math.

sections.title: Values have types
sections.body: In Python, every value has a type. Some types are immutable, like int and str. Others, like list, can change in place.
sections.tip: Type tells you what operations make sense.

sections.title: Casting is explicit
sections.body: Python will not guess when you are mixing text and numbers. Convert with int(), float(), str(), or bool() when you mean to change the representation.
sections.tip: Casting is usually the fix for beginner type errors.

sections.title: Mutability matters
sections.body: Lists and dictionaries can change after you pass them into a function. That is powerful, but it is also how hidden bugs show up when several parts of code share the same object.
sections.tip: Ask whether the object itself changes or just a name points elsewhere.

sections.title: None and truthiness
sections.body: None means "no value". It is not the same as 0 or an empty string, but all three are falsy in conditionals. That detail shows up constantly in real code.
sections.tip: Use is None, not == None.

keyTerms.definition: Cannot be changed in place after creation.
Can be changed in place.
Converting a value from one type to another.
A value treated as true in an if statement.
Python’s way of representing no value.

variables-types.feynmanPrompt: Explain the difference between mutating a list and rebinding a variable to a new object.

sections.title: Loops are controlled repetition
sections.body: A loop is just a way to repeat work until a condition changes. for-loops are for known sequences; while-loops are for conditions that need checking until they become false.
sections.tip: Choose the loop that matches the shape of the problem.

sections.title: Functions make logic reusable
sections.body: Functions let you name behavior. A good function has one clear job, takes the data it needs, and returns a result instead of hiding work in global state.
sections.tip: Prefer return values over hidden side effects.

sections.title: Parameters and defaults
sections.body: Default arguments are evaluated once when the function is defined, which is why mutable defaults are a common bug. *args and **kwargs let you accept flexible calling patterns.
sections.tip: Never use a list or dict as a default unless you know why.

sections.title: Lambda, break, and continue
sections.body: lambda is for tiny throwaway functions. break exits a loop immediately; continue skips the rest of the current iteration and moves to the next one.
sections.tip: The control-flow keyword should match the exact intent.

keyTerms.definition: A block of code that repeats.
Sends a value back from a function.
A named input to a function.
A value used when the caller does not supply one.
A short anonymous function.

loops-functions.feynmanPrompt: Explain why a function should usually return a value instead of printing one.

sections.title: Objects bundle state and behavior
sections.body: A class is a blueprint. An instance is a concrete object created from that blueprint. You use objects when you want data and the actions on that data to live together.
sections.tip: Ask what data belongs with the object.

sections.title: self is the instance
sections.body: self is just the instance being worked on. Methods read and change instance data through self, which is why you must include it explicitly in method signatures.
sections.tip: A method without self cannot see the instance.

sections.title: Inheritance and super()
sections.body: Inheritance lets one class reuse and extend another. super() calls the parent implementation so you can build on it instead of rewriting it from scratch.
sections.tip: Use inheritance when the child truly is a specialized version of the parent.

sections.title: Polymorphism and dunder methods
sections.body: Different objects can respond to the same operation in different ways. Dunder methods such as __len__ and __str__ let your custom objects fit naturally into Python conventions.
sections.tip: Polymorphism is why the same code can work on different types.

keyTerms.definition: A blueprint for making objects.
A concrete object created from a class.
A way to reuse and extend a parent class.
Keeping data and behavior together.
Different objects responding to the same operation.

oop.feynmanPrompt: Explain why self exists and what would break if you left it out of an instance method.

sections.title: Pick the right structure
sections.body: A data structure is a decision about how you want to organize and access data. The right choice depends on whether you care about ordering, uniqueness, random access, or fast lookup.
sections.tip: Choose the structure that makes the operation cheap.

sections.title: Lists and tuples
sections.body: Lists are mutable ordered sequences. Tuples are ordered too, but they are immutable. Use tuples when the data should not change.
sections.tip: If the collection should not be edited, tuple is often the cleaner choice.

sections.title: Sets and dictionaries
sections.body: Sets are for membership and uniqueness. Dictionaries are for key-value lookups. Both are hash-based, which is why membership checks are usually fast.
sections.tip: Use a dict when you need to map keys to values.

sections.title: Stacks, queues, and Big O
sections.body: A stack is LIFO. A queue is FIFO. Big O tells you how runtime or memory grows as the input size grows, which helps you compare tradeoffs.
sections.tip: Focus on growth, not exact milliseconds.

keyTerms.definition: An ordered, mutable sequence.
An ordered, immutable sequence.
An unordered collection of unique values.
A key-value mapping.
A way to describe how cost grows with input size.

data-structures.feynmanPrompt: Explain why you would choose a set over a list when checking if an item already exists.

sections.title: Complexity first
sections.body: Before you optimize code, ask how the cost grows as the input gets bigger. Big O is the language we use to compare algorithmic tradeoffs.
sections.tip: A slow algorithm on small input can still be the wrong design.

sections.title: Binary search
sections.body: Binary search only works on sorted data. Each step cuts the search space roughly in half, which is why it is so much faster than a linear scan.
sections.tip: If the input is not sorted, binary search is invalid.

sections.title: Sorting and recursion
sections.body: Merge sort splits the data, sorts each half, then merges them. Recursion is just a function solving a smaller version of the same problem until it hits a base case.
sections.tip: Every recursive function needs a base case.

sections.title: Two pointers
sections.body: Two-pointer techniques are useful when you can move from both ends of a structure toward the middle. They often turn an otherwise quadratic problem into a linear one.
sections.tip: Ask what invariant the pointers are maintaining.

keyTerms.definition: A step-by-step procedure for solving a problem.
The stopping condition in recursion.
A search strategy that halves the search space each step.
A divide-and-conquer sorting algorithm.
A technique using two indices to scan data efficiently.

algorithms.feynmanPrompt: Explain why binary search fails if the input is not sorted.

sections.title: Git records snapshots
sections.body: Git is not a file sync tool; it is a history machine. You make commits to capture snapshots of your project state so you can move around in time.
sections.code:
git init
git add .
git commit -m "start"
sections.tip: A commit should tell a story about one change.

sections.title: Branches let you experiment
sections.body: A branch is a movable pointer to a line of commits. Create branches when you want to work on a feature or fix without disturbing main.
sections.tip: Branches are cheap; use them freely.

sections.title: Merge, rebase, and conflicts
sections.body: Merge combines histories. Rebase rewrites your work onto another base. Conflicts happen when Git cannot decide how to combine changes automatically.
sections.code:
git merge main
git rebase main
sections.tip: Know when history is being rewritten.

sections.title: Recover safely
sections.body: Stash is for temporary shelving. reset moves branch pointers. pull brings remote changes down. push sends your commits up.
sections.code:
git stash
git pull --rebase
git push
sections.tip: Use reset with care because it can move history.

keyTerms.definition: A saved snapshot in Git history.
A movable pointer to a line of commits.
Combining work from different branches.
Replaying commits onto a new base.
Temporarily storing unfinished changes.

git-basics.feynmanPrompt: Explain the difference between merge and rebase in plain English.

sections.title: Navigate and inspect
sections.body: The shell is a text interface for interacting with files, processes, and programs. Start with ls, cd, pwd, and find until moving around feels automatic.
sections.tip: Know where you are before you change anything.

sections.title: Search with grep and find
sections.body: grep searches text. find searches the filesystem. Pipes let you connect commands so the output of one becomes the input of another.
sections.tip: Search problems often need both text and path filters.

sections.title: Permissions and processes
sections.body: chmod changes permissions. ps shows running processes. kill sends signals to stop them. The shell is often the quickest way to inspect what the system is doing.
sections.tip: Learn the common signals before you need them.

sections.title: Environment and remote work
sections.body: env prints environment variables, curl fetches URLs, and ssh opens remote sessions. These commands are the daily bread of many dev workflows.
sections.tip: A tiny command chain can replace a lot of manual clicking.

keyTerms.definition: Sends output from one command into another.
Whether a file can be read, written, or executed.
A running program.
A key-value setting available to programs.
A secure way to access a remote machine.

linux-cli.feynmanPrompt: Explain the difference between grep and find using a concrete example.

sections.title: What a network stack does
sections.body: Networking is the path from one program to another across machines. IP addresses identify hosts, ports identify processes, and protocols describe how messages are shaped and delivered.
sections.tip: Separate the machine from the app on that machine.

sections.title: DNS and IP
sections.body: DNS translates human-friendly names into IP addresses. Once you have the IP, the client can start talking to the server over the chosen protocol.
sections.tip: Name lookup happens before the real connection starts.

sections.title: TCP, UDP, and HTTP
sections.body: TCP is connection-oriented and reliable. UDP is lighter and faster but does not guarantee delivery. HTTP runs on top and defines request and response semantics.
sections.tip: Think reliability versus latency when comparing TCP and UDP.

sections.title: Localhost and debugging
sections.body: localhost points back to your own machine. ping checks reachability, traceroute shows the path, and curl is a simple way to inspect responses.
sections.tip: When a service fails, start by checking where the request stops.

keyTerms.definition: A numeric address for a networked host.
The endpoint number for a process on a machine.
The system that maps names to IP addresses.
A reliable transport protocol.
The protocol used for web requests and responses.

networking-basics.feynmanPrompt: Explain why a browser needs both DNS and ports before it can load a web page.

sections.title: Security starts with the CIA triad
sections.body: Confidentiality means only the right people can see the data. Integrity means the data was not altered unexpectedly. Availability means the system is there when you need it.
sections.tip: Use the triad to frame almost every security discussion.

sections.title: Hashing is not encryption
sections.body: Encryption is reversible with a key. Hashing is one-way and is used to verify data or store passwords more safely. You should never try to "decrypt" a hash.
sections.tip: Passwords are usually hashed, not encrypted.

sections.title: Common web attacks
sections.body: Phishing tricks people. SQL injection tricks databases. XSS tricks browsers. Each attack targets a different layer of the system, so defenses need to be layered too.
sections.code:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE name = ?
html.escape(user_input)
sections.tip: Input validation is not the same as output encoding.

sections.title: Passwords and MFA
sections.body: Good password security uses long, unique passwords stored in a password manager. MFA adds another factor, which dramatically raises the cost of account takeover.
sections.code: password manager -> unique password -> MFA code
sections.tip: A leaked password is much less dangerous when MFA is enabled.

keyTerms.definition: Confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
A one-way transformation used for integrity or password storage.
Social engineering that tries to trick someone into revealing secrets.
A browser attack that injects malicious script into a page.
Multi-factor authentication.

cybersecurity-101.feynmanPrompt: Explain why hashing passwords is safer than storing them in plain text.
